Aneena Shakeer • Priya K. Nair • Renju  Jose  • Mahija Janardhanan • Aravind M S

Aneena Shakeer • Priya K. Nair • Renju Jose • Mahija Janardhanan • Aravind M S

Cite this article as: Shakeer A, Nair P K, Jose R, et al. (April 09, 2026) Oral Squamous Papilloma of the Lower Labial Mucosa: A Case Report. Cureus 18(4): e106734. doi:10.7759/cureus.106734

Oral squamous papilloma (OSP) is a benign epithelial proliferation commonly encountered in the oral cavity and often presents as an exophytic growth with a papillary surface architecture. Although typically non-aggressive, its clinical resemblance to other verrucopapillary lesions necessitates careful diagnostic evaluation.

This report describes a case of OSP in a 58-year-old male who presented to a tertiary care center in Kerala, India, with a history of chronic tobacco smoking and an incidental exophytic lesion on the lower labial mucosa. The lesion was surgically excised using a diode laser, and histopathological examination confirmed the diagnosis.

Early recognition and histopathological confirmation are essential for appropriate management and to rule out potentially malignant lesions, especially in patients with risk-modifying habits such as tobacco use.

Oral squamous papilloma (OSP) is a benign proliferative lesion of stratified squamous epithelium characterized by papillary or verruciform exophytic growth [1]. It represents one of the most common benign epithelial tumors of the oral cavity and accounts for approximately 2-4% of all biopsied oral lesions [2]. Clinically, it presents as a solitary, slow-growing, painless mass with a pedunculated or sessile base and a characteristic cauliflower-like surface morphology [3].

The etiopathogenesis of OSP is strongly associated with human papillomavirus (HPV), particularly low-risk genotypes HPV-6 and HPV-11 [4]. HPV is a double-stranded deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) virus with epithelial tropism that infects basal keratinocytes through micro-abrasions in the mucosa [5]. Viral replication is closely linked to epithelial differentiation, resulting in hyperplastic epithelial proliferation and the formation of papillary projections [6]. Unlike high-risk HPV types (16 and 18), which integrate into host DNA and disrupt tumor suppressor pathways such as p53 and pRb, low-risk types typically remain episomal and are associated with benign lesions [7].

Recent literature has increasingly emphasized the clinical relevance of HPV in oral lesions rather than detailed molecular mechanisms, particularly in benign conditions such as OSP. The reported prevalence of HPV in oral papillomas varies widely depending on detection methods and population characteristics, with studies from Asian populations demonstrating comparable but heterogeneous trends. In addition to viral factors, local environmental influences such as tobacco exposure play an important modifying role, contributing to epithelial alterations that may facilitate viral persistence and lesion development. In regions such as India, where tobacco use is prevalent, these factors are particularly relevant in interpreting oral mucosal lesions [5,8,9].

Histopathologically, OSP demonstrates finger-like projections of hyperparakeratinized stratified squamous epithelium supported by fibrovascular connective tissue cores [8]. Koilocytosis, characterized by perinuclear halos and nuclear hyperchromasia, may be observed, reflecting HPV-induced cytopathic changes [9]. Mitotic activity is generally confined to the basal layer, and cellular atypia is absent in typical cases [10].

Epidemiologically, OSP can occur across a broad age range but is most commonly reported in adults between the third and sixth decades of life [2,11]. There is no consistent gender predilection, though some studies report slight female predominance [1]. In pediatric populations, OSP constitutes approximately 7-8% of oral tumors [12]. While lesions in adults are typically solitary, multiple lesions may be observed more frequently in children and immunocompromised individuals [13,14]. This variation highlights differences in host immune response and viral behavior across age groups, contributing to distinct clinical presentations.

Common intraoral sites include the tongue, soft palate, uvula, lips, and labial mucosa [15-17]. Despite its benign nature, the clinical significance of OSP lies in its resemblance to other verrucopapillary lesions, necessitating careful differential diagnosis. Lesions such as verruca vulgaris, condyloma acuminatum, focal epithelial hyperplasia, verruciform xanthoma, papillary hyperplasia, and early verrucous carcinoma may present with overlapping clinical features, making histopathological confirmation essential [18,19].

Risk factors contributing to OSP development include HPV exposure, tobacco use, alcohol consumption, chronic irritation, and immunosuppression [15]. Tobacco smoking, in particular, has been associated with increased epithelial susceptibility to viral persistence and mucosal alterations such as melanosis and keratinization [16]. Although malignant transformation of OSP is exceedingly rare, persistent HPV infection and synergistic carcinogenic factors warrant careful evaluation [17].

Management primarily involves complete surgical excision with narrow margins [20]. Alternative treatment modalities include laser ablation, electrocautery, and cryotherapy [21]. Recurrence is uncommon and typically associated with incomplete removal or persistent viral infection [22]. Prognosis is excellent in immunocompetent individuals [3].

The present report describes a case of OSP in a chronic smoker, emphasizing clinical features, diagnostic approach, histopathological correlation, and management considerations.

A 58-year-old male patient reported to a tertiary care center in Kochi, Kerala, India, with the chief complaint of multiple decayed teeth in the lower posterior region for one year and missing teeth in both upper and lower arches for three to four years. The patient’s primary concern was a loosened tooth and prosthetic rehabilitation. Medical and family history were non-contributory. The patient reported a history of smoking 20 cigarettes per day for approximately 13 years and occasional alcohol consumption. No history of systemic illness or immunocompromised state was elicited. Extraoral examination revealed no significant changes.

Intraoral examination revealed diffuse greyish-black pigmentation, suggestive of smoker’s melanosis on the lower labial mucosa. Incidentally, a solitary exophytic growth was observed on the lower labial mucosa in relation to teeth 31 and 41. The lesion measured approximately 0.5 × 0.6 cm. It was pedunculated, non-tender, non-scrapable, and exhibited a cauliflower-like surface morphology. No bleeding, ulceration, or discharge was present. The surrounding mucosa appeared otherwise normal except for pigmentation. The clinical presentation is outlined in Figure 1.

A solitary, well-defined, pedunculated exophytic lesion with a cauliflower-like surface morphology is seen on the lower labial mucosa in relation to teeth 31 and 41. The lesion appears non-ulcerated and non-erythematous, with surrounding mucosa showing features of smoker’s melanosis.

Based on clinical presentation, a provisional diagnosis of squamous papilloma was made. Differential diagnoses included verruca vulgaris, condyloma acuminatum, focal epithelial hyperplasia, and early verrucous carcinoma.

Routine hematological investigations were performed before the biopsy. Table 1 summarizes the patient’s preoperative hematological parameters. All values were within normal physiological limits.

An excisional biopsy was planned. Under local anesthesia, the lesion was completely excised using a diode laser, which offers advantages such as reduced intraoperative bleeding, better visibility, and minimal postoperative discomfort. The surgical site was irrigated with povidone-iodine and saline. Suturing was performed to approximate wound margins. This is shown in Figure 2.

Postoperative view of the lower labial mucosa following diode laser excision, showing satisfactory wound healing with no evidence of bleeding, infection, or residual lesion.

The excised specimen was submitted for histopathological evaluation. This is shown in Figure 3.

Gross specimen of the excised lesion placed on sterile gauze, showing a small, soft tissue mass with papillary surface features consistent with a clinical diagnosis of oral squamous papilloma.

Upon microscopic examination, an exophytic lesion characterized by hyperplastic superficial stratified squamous epithelium thrown into numerous papilliferous surface projections was seen. The epithelium shows parakeratosis. A prominent granular layer was noticed. Focal koilocytic change was noticed within the epithelium. No evidence of dysplasia or malignant transformation was observed, confirming the diagnosis of OSP. The histopathologic representation is shown in Figures 4-6.

H&E-stained section at 4x magnification illustrating a pedunculated exophytic growth. The lesion exhibits multiple finger-like papilliferous surface projections composed of hyperplastic stratified squamous epithelium. Each projection is supported by a thin, central core of vascularized fibrovascular connective tissue (blue arrow) extending from the underlying lamina propria.

H&E-stained section at 20x magnification showing the structural detail of the papillary stalks. The epithelium displays prominent acanthosis and a well-defined basal cell layer. The fibrovascular connective tissue is continuous with the stalk of the lesion, while the papilliferous surface (red arrow) shows evidence of orthokeratosis or parakeratosis, typical of oral squamous papilloma.

H&E-stained section at 40x magnification, focusing on the spinous and granular layers of the epithelium. Numerous koilocytes (yellow arrow) are visible, characterized by pyknotic, hyperchromatic nuclei surrounded by a clear perinuclear cytoplasmic halo. Koilocytic changes observed within the epithelium are suggestive of HPV-associated cytopathic effect; however, definitive confirmation requires molecular diagnostic techniques such as polymerase chain reaction (PCR), in situ hybridization (ISH), or immunohistochemistry.

Postoperative medications included analgesics and an antiseptic mouth rinse for seven days. The patient was advised to undergo smoking cessation counselling. Follow-up at one week showed satisfactory healing, and complete mucosal recovery was observed subsequently. No recurrence was noted. The clinical timeline is detailed in Figure 7.

This figure illustrates the sequence of clinical events, including initial presentation with dental complaints, incidental detection of the lesion, excisional biopsy using a diode laser, and satisfactory postoperative healing. The timeline was created using Microsoft PowerPoint (Microsoft Corporation, Redmond, WA, USA).

OSP is a benign epithelial neoplasm characterized by papillary proliferation of stratified squamous epithelium, most commonly associated with low-risk HPV genotypes [1,4]. It constitutes a significant proportion of oral epithelial proliferations and remains an important diagnostic consideration in verrucopapillary lesions of the oral cavity [2]. Although biologically benign, its clinical resemblance to potentially malignant disorders needs careful evaluation.

The etiopathogenesis of OSP is closely linked to HPV infection, particularly genotypes 6 and 11 [4]. These low-risk viral types infect basal keratinocytes through micro-abrasions in the mucosal epithelium and replicate in synchrony with epithelial differentiation [6]. The viral genome persists episomally rather than integrating into host DNA, which explains the non-aggressive biological behavior of the lesion [7]. The E6 and E7 oncoproteins produced by high-risk HPV strains (16 and 18) are known to inactivate tumor suppressor proteins p53 and retinoblastoma (pRb), facilitating oncogenesis [17]. However, such oncogenic integration is rarely observed in solitary OSP, reinforcing its benign nature.

In the present case, the lesion was identified while evaluating decayed teeth. This aligns with the typical clinical presentation of OSP, which is often asymptomatic and slow-growing [3]. Abbey et al. analyzed 464 cases and reported that most lesions were less than 1 cm in diameter and predominantly solitary [2]. The size of the lesion in our patient (0.5 × 0.6 cm) falls within this commonly reported range. The lower labial mucosa, though less frequently involved than the tongue and soft palate, remains a recognized site [13].

The patient’s history of chronic tobacco smoking is clinically significant. Tobacco exposure induces epithelial hyperplasia, keratinization, and pigmentation changes such as smoker’s melanosis [15,16]. Chronic exposure to tobacco carcinogens may compromise local immune surveillance and epithelial integrity, potentially facilitating viral persistence [9]. While smoking itself is not a direct etiologic factor for OSP, it acts as a modifying cofactor in epithelial pathology and increases the overall risk of malignant transformation in the oral cavity [15]. Therefore, histopathological confirmation is mandatory in smokers presenting with verrucous lesions.

The role of tobacco smoking in OSP warrants further clinical consideration. While tobacco is not a direct etiological agent in the development of OSP, it functions as a significant modifying cofactor by altering the local mucosal microenvironment. Chronic exposure to tobacco smoke leads to epithelial hyperkeratinization, increased mucosal permeability, and microvascular changes, which may facilitate viral entry and persistence. Additionally, tobacco-related immunomodulation impairs local immune surveillance by reducing Langerhans cell activity and altering cytokine profiles, thereby diminishing the host’s ability to clear HPV infection effectively [9,16]. This compromised immune response may prolong viral persistence and contribute to lesion development or delayed resolution. Therefore, in patients with a history of tobacco use, OSP should be interpreted within a broader context of epithelial vulnerability and increased oncogenic risk, necessitating careful histopathological evaluation and long-term monitoring.

Differential diagnosis remains critical due to overlapping clinical features among verrucopapillary lesions. Verruca vulgaris, commonly caused by HPV-2 and HPV-4, typically exhibits more pronounced hyperkeratosis and elongated rete ridges converging toward the center [18]. Condyloma acuminatum, usually associated with sexual transmission, often presents as multiple lesions with broader and blunter papillary projections [19]. Verrucous carcinoma and early exophytic squamous cell carcinoma must be excluded due to their malignant potential; these lesions exhibit invasive growth patterns, dysplasia, and pushing borders histologically [18]. Table 2 summarizes key distinguishing features to aid in the clinical and histopathological differentiation of common verrucopapillary lesions of the oral cavity.

Microscopic examination of hematoxylin and eosin-stained sections revealed a well-circumscribed, exophytic lesion composed of hyperplastic stratified squamous epithelium arranged in multiple finger-like papilliferous projections. Each projection was supported by a central fibrovascular connective tissue core continuous with the underlying lamina propria [9,22]. The surface epithelium exhibited predominantly parakeratinization, with focal areas of orthokeratinization. A prominent granular cell layer and acanthosis were evident. The rete ridges appeared elongated and mildly pointed, without dysplastic architectural disturbance. Focal koilocytic changes were observed within the superficial epithelial layers, characterized by perinuclear clearing and nuclear hyperchromasia. These features are suggestive of HPV-associated cytopathic effect, although not diagnostic in the absence of molecular confirmation [10].

The basement membrane remained intact, with no evidence of epithelial invasion into the underlying connective tissue. Mitotic figures were sparse and confined to the basal layer, with no atypical mitoses identified. The underlying connective tissue stroma showed a mild chronic inflammatory infiltrate composed predominantly of lymphocytes. Importantly, no features of epithelial dysplasia or malignant transformation were observed. Based on these findings, a definitive diagnosis of OSP was established [23].

Laser excision was chosen in this case due to its well-documented advantages. Laser surgery provides superior hemostasis, reduced postoperative edema, decreased bacterial contamination, and minimal scarring [21]. Studies have demonstrated favorable healing outcomes and low recurrence rates following laser excision of benign oral lesions [21]. Conventional scalpel excision remains the gold standard and is equally effective when complete removal with adequate margins is achieved [20]. Recurrence rates for solitary OSP are low, generally attributed to incomplete excision or persistent viral infection [22].

Malignant transformation of solitary OSP is exceedingly rare [17]. However, persistent HPV infection, immunosuppression, and synergistic carcinogenic exposures such as tobacco and alcohol may theoretically increase oncogenic risk [24,25]. Gillison et al. highlighted the growing role of HPV in oropharyngeal carcinomas, particularly HPV-16 [24,26]. Although the pathogenic pathways differ between low-risk and high-risk HPV types, clinicians must remain vigilant when evaluating papillomatous lesions in high-risk individuals.

The present case reinforces the importance of comprehensive oral examination, even when patients present for unrelated dental complaints. Incidental findings can represent clinically significant pathology requiring intervention. Although OSP is a common benign lesion, this case is notable for its incidental detection in a chronic smoker with adjacent smoker’s melanosis, highlighting the importance of thorough oral examination even in patients presenting with unrelated complaints. The case also emphasizes the role of tobacco-induced epithelial alterations as a potential modifying factor in lesion development. Furthermore, successful management using diode laser excision demonstrates a minimally invasive approach with favorable clinical outcomes. Thus, the significance of this report lies in its contextual presentation in a high-risk individual, its incidental discovery, and its effective management, reinforcing the need for clinical vigilance in routine dental practice [25,26].

From a public health perspective, HPV-related oral lesions underscore the broader implications of viral oncogenesis. Prophylactic HPV vaccination has demonstrated efficacy in reducing infection with high-risk genotypes and may potentially reduce the incidence of HPV-associated oral lesions in the future [25,26]. Although vaccination primarily targets cervical and anogenital HPV infection, its expanding role in head and neck oncology is increasingly recognized.

Smoking cessation counseling was advised for this patient to mitigate the overall oral cancer risk. Tobacco cessation not only reduces epithelial dysplastic changes but also enhances mucosal immunity and wound healing. Regular follow-up remains essential to monitor for recurrence or development of additional lesions.

In summary, OSP is a benign HPV-associated epithelial proliferation with characteristic clinical and histological features. Despite its non-aggressive behavior, accurate differentiation from potentially malignant lesions is critical. Complete excision, histopathological confirmation, patient education, and risk factor modification collectively ensure optimal prognosis.

This case report has certain limitations that must be acknowledged. First, the absence of definitive HPV confirmation through molecular diagnostic methods such as polymerase chain reaction (PCR), in situ hybridization (ISH), or immunohistochemistry (IHC) limits the ability to establish a direct etiological association between the lesion and HPV. Although histopathological features such as koilocytosis were observed, these findings are suggestive but not diagnostic of HPV infection. Second, while the histopathological evaluation was comprehensive, advanced ancillary investigations, such as immunohistochemical markers, were not performed. Inclusion of such markers could have provided additional insight into epithelial proliferative activity and helped further differentiate benign from potentially dysplastic or malignant lesions. Third, it represents a single-patient observation, which limits the generalizability of the findings. Fourth, although short-term postoperative healing was satisfactory, long-term follow-up data beyond the initial mucosal recovery period were not available, restricting assessment of recurrence or late complications. Additionally, advanced diagnostic modalities such as HPV typing or immunohistochemical analysis were not performed, which could have provided further insight into the viral characteristics and biological behavior of the lesion. Future studies with larger sample sizes and extended follow-up are necessary to better understand the clinical implications and recurrence patterns of OSP.

OSP is a benign HPV-associated epithelial proliferation that may clinically mimic other verrucopapillary lesions. Accurate diagnosis requires thorough clinical examination and histopathological confirmation. Complete surgical excision provides definitive treatment with an excellent prognosis. In patients with tobacco exposure, careful evaluation and habit counseling are essential to reduce potential oncogenic risk. Early detection remains the cornerstone of optimal management.

KKR vs LSG highlights IPL 2026: Mukul Choudhary helps Lucknow Super Giants seal a thriller at Eden Gardens - Sportstar
content:
KKR were firm favourites to win the match but Mukul Choudhary played a knock for the ages to help LSG cross the line. | Photo Credit: MOORTHY RV

Welcome to the highlights of the IPL 2026 match between Kolkata Knight Riders and Lucknow Super Giants at the Eden Gardens in Kolkata on Thursday.

What a thriller. Mukul Choudhary won the game for LSG out of nowhere. 54 off 27 balls with the help of seven sixes and two fours. Absolutely stunning cameo by the 21-year-old. KKR remains winless after four matches.

14 runs needed off the last over. Avesh Khan is on strike. Vaibhav Arora to bowl the final over.

Mukul on strike. 13 needed off five balls. SIX!!!! Mukul moves across and pulls it over square leg for a maximum. Huge hit!! 7 off 4 balls now.

Yorker and Mukul misses it! A dot ball. Brilliant execution by Vaibhav. 7 off 3 balls.

Moves across, Vaibhav bowls a yorker and Mukul misses again! Wanted to ramp the ball. 7 off 2 balls.

SIX!!! Full ball and Mukul lofts the ball over covers for a maximum!! What a fantastic shot. Brilliant wrist work.

Bouncer, Mukul misses but Avesh runs quickly and gets to the crease. LSG has won this out of nowhere! Raghuvanshi missed his direct hit. What a match!

Green for the penultimate over. Mukul skies the first ball high in the air but the ball falls short of the long off fielder! A dot.

Another dot, as Mukul misses a pull this time. Now he nails his pull and it’s a six! Went just over the square leg fence. Brilliant batting.

Slower delivery, Mukul is beaten; a swing and a miss. FOUR! Another wild swing and he gets a top edge over keeper and ball goes for four.

SIX!! Finishes the over with a humongous six. Pulled over midwicket.

LSG needs 43 off the last three overs. Kartik Tyagi to bowl.

SIX! Short ball and another helicopter sort of a hit but over long off this time. Brilliant hitting; what wrists!

Doesn’t run on the second delivery. Hard length, Mukul once again doesn’t run even as he whacks it towards midwicket.

SIX!! Full toss and punished. Launched way into the long on stand! Brilliant hitting this.

Now he takes a single off the fifth delivery towards midwicket. Avesh Khan’s wild swing and miss to end the over.

Vaibhav is back. Mukul pulls but doesn’t take a single. He’s farming the strike. Another dot, this time he’s beaten by pace.

Mukul pulls and this time he finds the fence. Good effort by Ramandeep at square leg but can’t stop the boundary.

SIX! What a brilliant shot...helicopter shot! Full ball is whacked into the long on stands. Full toss, Mukul can’t time the ball and only gets a single.

Sunil Narine. A dot to begin with. Ball is turning and Mukul has been beaten twice in two deliveries. Mukul doesn’t seem to have a clue what Narine is bowling. Three dots.

Now Mukul sweeps and takes a single. Now Shami is beaten by the turn. Shami swings, and the ball lands straight into the hands of the fielder at deep square leg. LSG loses its seventh wicket.

Anukul begins with a couple of dot balls. Now he bowls a full toss and Badoni smacks it over long on for a six! Terrific way to get to his fifty. Important knock.

OUT! Badoni falls next ball. Wide delivery, Badoni went for the slog but doesn’t it well and is caught at long off. LSG in a great spot of bother. Six down and with no specialist batters left.

Mohammad Shami is the new man in. Takes a single first ball.

Navdeep is back. Badoni lofts over covers and gets a four. Picked the slower one quickly and nails the execution. Follows it up with a brilliant cover drive for four! Another slower one.

Another slower delivery but this stays low and Badoni can’t slash it. Short ball, Badoni cuts past point and get two.

Four! Now Badoni slashes in the air and it goes over the point region and bounces just before the ropes. Brilliant shot.

Anukul Roy. Samad takes a single off the first ball. Wide but just inside the line and Badoni ends up playing a dot.

Now Badoni sweeps and perfectly places it towards deep square leg region for four. Now a wide ball.

Full toss, Badoni lofts it and ball just falls before the long off fielder. OUT! Samad is gone. A wild swing but too early by Samad as the ball crashes into the stumps. KKR in front. Samad goes for 2 off 4 balls.

Kartik Tyagi for this second. Begins with a dot as Badoni is beaten trying to slash. Now a bouncer and Badoni upper cuts and it goes for four! Easy pickings.

Badoni works the ball towards leg side and has to settle for a single. OUT! Pooran tries to slog a short ball and is caught at long on! Poor shot selection. It was the pace off delivery as well. Pooran has to go after scoring 13 off 15 balls.

Abdul Samad is the new man in. A swing and a miss off the first ball. Takes a single next ball.

Navdeep Saini is back. Slower delivery, Pooran drives towards covers - a dot. Another slower delivery, Pooran swings and misses. Short ball but too high, a wide.

Pooran drives wide of long off and takes a double. Another slower one and Pooran plays another dot. Another slower one, another dot. Pooran isn’t able to time them.

Now Pooran drives towards deep cover and finishes the over with a double.

Narine with his third. Pooran on strike, slip in place. Oooohhh, beats Pooran with a carrom ball, justttt misses the top of off stump.

Pooran defends that, dot ball. Oooohhh, beats Pooran’s defence and misses his outside edge by a whisker again. Pooran gets off strike with a single on the leg side.

Carrom ball by Narine, Badoni outside edges it to short point for a dot ball. Badoni defends, dot ball. Another tidy over from Narine, just one run off that.

Match hanging in the balance. Cameron Green is into the attack, he’s finally bowling. Badoni welcomes him with a cut to deep point for a single. WHAT A START TO CAMERON GREEN’S BOWLING SPELL! Well directed bouncer and Pant can only top edge it to Kartik Tyagi at short fine leg! PANT IS GONE! HUGE MOMENT IN THE GAME!

Rishabh Pant c Kartik Tyagi b Cameron Green 10(9) [4s-1]

Nicholas Pooran in at four for LSG. That’s too short from Green, wide given. Small pause in action, Green changes his boots. Action resumes. Pooran off the mark with a single on the off side.

Badoni cross bats that to mid on for a single. Pooran pulls it jusssstttt over deep cover for a SIX! A tall fielder there and Pooran was gone.

That’s too short again from Green, second short ball of the over and that’s a free hit! Pooran can only top edge it straight to Powell’s hands on the free hit, takes a single.

Narine with his second. Pant cuts it to deep point for a single. Badoni tries to go on the off side but inside edges it straight back to the bowler, dot ball. Badoni guides it off his pads to the leg side for a single.

Pant hits it off his pads to deep square leg for a single. Again, single on the leg side from Badoni. Oooohh that was close, picks Pant’s outside edge and goes to short third, dot ball.

PowerPlay done, Kartik Tyagi to bowl the seventh. Nice shot from Badoni again, welcomes Tyagi with a square drive for a BOUNDARY!

Well directed short ball from Tyagi, Badoni defends and takes a quick single. Pant hits it off his pads to deep square leg for a single.

Wide of off stump from Tyagi, Badoni hits it to deep cover for a single. Pant comes down the ground and plays a beautiful cover drive for a BOUNDARY!

Pant tries to go over short fine leg but the ball goes to the leg side for a single.

Sunil Narine into the attack. Badoni defends. Carrom ball from Narine, Badoni hits it off his back foot to long on for a single. LSG going at just above the required run rate of 9.

Pant takes a single on the leg side. Nice shot from Badoni, hits it off his back foot through the off side for a BOUNDARY!

Oooohhh, that hits Badoni’s pads, no appeal from KKR though. Badoni tugs it to the leg side for a single.

Vaibhav Arora back into the attack. Short ball from Vaibhav, Markram tries to pull but top edges that straight to deep mid wicket and Rovman Powell takes the catch! MARKRAM IS GONE! WICKET!

Aiden Markram c Rovman Powell b Vaibhav Arora 22(15) [4s-2 6s-1]

Captain Rishabh Pant is in at three. Much need breakthrough to get rid of Markram for KKR. Slip in place. Pant gets off the mark with a single on the leg side.

Bouncer from Arora and is that gone?!!! YES IT IS! MARSH TRIES TO PULL IT BUT IT NICKS OFF HIS GLOVES AND GOES TO THE KEEPER! DOUBLE WICKET OVER FROM ARORA!

Mitchell Marsh c Angkrish Raghuvanshi b Vaibhav Arora 15(11) [4s-1 6s-1]

Badoni is the impact sub and he is in at 4, M Siddharth is subbed out. Short ball from Vaibhav and Badoni lets it alone, umpire signals first bounce.

Oooohhh that could have easily been three in the same over! Badoni pulls it just over deep fine leg for a SIX! Lucky for Badoni. Vaibhav goes short again, Badoni pulls it to deep fine leg, one bounce. What a gamechanging over from Vaibhav.

Anukul with his second. Marsh off his back foot hits it to long off for a single. Oooohhh arm ball and that skids through and keeps a little low, Markram does well to get some bat on that, takes a single on the leg side.

Full from Anukul, Marsh hits it to long off for a single. Markram tries to go down the ground but it hits the inside half of his bat and goes to square leg, dot ball. Anukul tosses that up nicely, Markram hits it to long off for a single.

Marsh sweeps it for a SIX! Such a good over from Anukul until that point but LSG finish on a high.

Navdeep Saini into the attack, only that one over for Vaibhav Arora. Markram on strike. Plays his trademark pick up shot lifting his back leg over mid on for a BOUNDARY!

Markram goes again, same shot but its timed even better this time, GOES ALL THE WAY FOR SIX! Slower ball from Saini but Markram finds the gap through cover for a BOUNDARY! Bowling change has not worked for KKR.

Slower ball, hits Markram’s pads but that’s missing the stumps, one leg bye. Another slower ball, Marsh cuts that to point for a single on the up. Markram guides it off his pads to deep square leg for a single. Good over for LSG, 18 came off that.

Anukul Roy to bowl the second, spin in the second over. Markram hits it to long off for a single. That’s wide down the leg side. Marsh defends. Arm ball from Anukul, Marsh hits it to long off for a single. Good start with the ball so far from KKR.

Markram punches it off his back foot to deep mid wicket for a single. Marsh tries to sweep that but doesn’t connect, hits his pads and goes to short fine leg, one leg bye. Wide down the leg side again, he’ll have to bowl that again. Markram hits it to long off for a single. Good first over from Anukul, no boundaries conceded.

Marsh and Markram walk out to the middle to open the batting for LSG. Vaibhav Arora takes the new ball for KKR, who is the impact sub. Marsh on strike, starts with a BOUNDARY! CUTS THAT FOR FOUR!

Good inswinger close to the body from Arora, Marsh defends and its a dot ball. Another inswinger, Marsh hits it to mid off, slight hesitation for the run but he eventually takes the single. Markram defends to mid wicket, dot ball.

Inswinger again from Arora, Markram hits it to cover, dot ball. Markram guides it off his pads to mid on for a single.

KKR looked set for a 200 plus score after the tenth over but LSG’s pace bowlers nailed their yorkers to perfection in the back end of the innings to restrict the host to 184. Can LSG’s mighty batting lineup chase this down or can KKR’s bowlers pull off something special here? Second innings to begin shortly.

Two very different halves for Kolkata Knight Riders. In the first 10 overs, Knight Riders were 102 but they lost three consecutive wickets off well set batters: Rahane, Raghuvanshi, and Rinku Singh. Cameron Green struggled to time the ball while Powell swung hard from the other end. However, some good defensive bowling helped Super Giants restrict KKR under 200. Maybe, 15 runs short. We’ll find out soon.

Prince for the last over. Low full toss, Powell ramps it towards fine leg region for four! Smart hit. Short ball and is called a wide. Another wide, this time by LSG. Umpire calls it a wide.

Yorker, and Powell can only take a single. Green slogs, gets an inside edge and it goes for four! Another yorker, this time Green can only take a single.

A wild swing and a miss by Powell. A pace off delivery by Prince there. Another slower ball, Powell misses again and Green runs quickly to take a single. Splendid last over by Prince.

Shami to bowl the penultimate over. A single via leg-bye. Green on strike now.

SIX! In the slot and Green launches the ball over long on. Nicely timed that. Short ball, Green misses it. Umpire doesn’t give it wide and Green once again wants to review it. It’s a wide, above head. Green takes a single next ball.

Yorker and Powell manages to sneak a single. Another yorker, Green plays it nicely and takes a single towards point region. Another yorker, Powell works it towards leg side for a single.

Avesh. Green takes a single first ball. Powell doesn’t time his pull and can only take a double.

Low full toss and Powell smacks it towards wide of long on for four! Avesh bowls a wide delivery.

Another low full toss but only a single this time for Powell. Short ball, Green misses the slash. Green has reviewed for a wide ball. It’s a fair delivery.

Full ball and Green smacks it away for four towards long on to end the over.

Prince is back for his third over. A yorker to begin with; Green does well to play it and takes a single. Powell takes a single the next ball; another reverse swinging yorker by Prince.

Short ball, Green upper cuts and in the gap between short third and point for four. A quick single next ball.

Full and Powell smacks it straight back past the bowler for four! Powerfully struck. And he ends the over with a six! Full toss tonked away towards midwicket stand.

Siddarth for his final over. Powell comes down the track and launches the ball way back into the long on stand! Huge six and a much needed one.

Siddarth follows it up with a yorker and Powell defends it back. Now Powell gets an inside edge and it goes for four behind the keeper! A turnaround over for KKR?

A dot next ball as Powell hits straight back to the bowler. Now a single towards deep midwicket.

Digvesh for his final over. Powell takes a single off the first ball. Green plays a couple of dots next two balls.

Defends the third ball in a row, Green. Works the ball towards long on for a single off the fifth delivery of the over.

Avesh Khan is back. Rinku drives straight to cover, a dot to begin the over.

OUT! Rinku moves back a bit, misses the line completely and is cleaned up. Wanted to cut it but was too late to put the bat behind the ball. He falls for just 4 off 7 balls.

Rovman Powell in at six. A dot to begin. Short ball, Powell wanted to work it towards leg side but can’t time the ball.

Another short ball, Powell attempts a pull but misses. Another dot. Third dot in a row. This time Powell gets an inside edge and is lucky not to chop it on.

A single to end the over. Just one run and a wicket in that over.

Shami is back. Rinku wants to slash, gets an inside edge but takes a single anyway. Green drives the low full toss towards long off for a single.

Rinku now cuts towards deep third for a single. Green gives him the strike back next ball.

Slower delivery and Rinku plays a dot, driving straight to cover fielder. A single to end the over.

Siddharth with his third over. Green takes a single towards deep covers. Raghuvanshi gives him the strike back.

OUT! Raghuvanshi comes down the track and is caught at long on. Lofts it straight to the fielder. Raghuvanshi falls after scoring 45. He’s mighty disappointed with himself.

Back-to-back wickets for LSG. Rinku Singh at the crease now. He takes a single off the first ball.

Digvesh for his third over. Raghuvanshi takes a single off the first ball.

OUT! Rahane pulls a short delivery straight to short midwicket. Shami, the fielder, thinks that catch is clean. It was a low diving effort. Rahane has been asked to wait. And after several replays, umpire has given him out! Rahane falls for 41 off 24 balls. Digvesh with the breakthrough.

Cam Green at four. A dot first up. Drives the second delivery and takes a single towards long off.

Raghuvanshi gives Green the strike back the next ball. A single to end the over by Green.

Prince Yadav with his second. Rahane pulls it on the leg side for a single. That’s too short from Prince, wide is called, he’ll have to bowl that again.

Full from Prince, Raghuvanshi hits that straight to the bowler for a dot ball. Raghuvanshi guides it to deep square leg for a single. Slower ball from Prince, Rahane hits it straight for a single.

Full toss from Prince, Raghuvanshi plays it fine and it beats deep fine leg for a BOUNDARY! Short ball from Prince, Raghuvanshi pulls it to the leg side for a single.

Avesh Khan back into the attack. Rahane slashes that on the bounce, loses his bat but the ball travels all the way on the off side for a SIX! WHAT A BIZZARE SHOT!

Full from Avesh, Rahane crisply hits that to deep cover for a single. Change of pace from Avesh, Raghuvanshi guides that on the leg side for a double.

WHAT A SHOT FROM RAGHUVANSHI! FLICKS THAT EFFORTLESSLY OFF HIS PADS FOR A SIX! Raghuvanshi drives that well but its straight to extra-cover, dot ball. Change of pace from Avesh again, another dot ball to finish.

Rathi to bowl the 8th over. Rahane chips that in the air to long off for a single. Raghuvanshi tries to reverse sweep it but it hits his pads, LSG decide against taking the review. That looked like it was outside the line.

Rahane flicks that beautifully in the gap between long on and deep mid wicket for a FOUR! BEAUTIFULLY TIMED FOR RAHANE!

Rahane guides that towards the leg side for single. Raghuvanshi hits that to deep cover for a single. Rahane chips that in the air straight down the ground for a single.

Siddharth with his second. Rahane guides it off his pads on the leg side for a single. Raghuvanshi times that well on the off side for two runs. Raghuvanshi sweeps it and beats the fielder at deep square leg for a BOUNDARY!

Good recovery from KKR this after losing Allen early. Raghuvanshi cuts it to Marsh at short point for a single. Rahane off his pads hits it to short fine leg for a single, 50 partnership up between the pair. Raghuvanshi hits it straight to extra cover, dot ball.

Avesh Khan to bowl the sixth. Starts well, beats Raghuvanshi’s outside edge. Raghuvanshi does not connect again but sneaks in a single, he’s scored only six runs from his 12 balls so far.

Full toss from Avesh, Rahane drives that beautifully but some brilliant fielding at cover by Mukul Choudhary, he’s saved three runs for his team.

Raghuvanshi on the bounce slices it over short third for a BOUNDARY! This will make him feel good. Avesh goes too full and Raghuvanshi hits it over cover all the way for SIX! WHAT A SHOT! Another good shot from Raghuvanshi, threads it through the off side for another BOUNDARY!

Digvesh Rathi into the attack, spin from both ends. Starts with a short one outside off stump, Rahane fails to put that away, dot ball. Rahane flicks it straight to square leg for a dot.

Another short one from Rathi, Rahane tries to pull it but inside edges the ball onto his pads, dot ball again. Rahane creates room and goes down the ground, doesn’t get all of it but the ball trickles to the boundary! FOUR RUNS!

Rahane creates room but slices it, goes onto the practice wickets behind short third and the ball races away for ANOTHER BOUNDARY! Rahane flicks it to long on for a double.

M Siddharth into the attack, spin introduced in the PowerPlay. Raghuvanshi starts with a dot. Wide down leg side, Pant does not collect it and KKR takes a single.

Rahane off his back foot his it to square leg, dot ball. Rahane comes down the ground and lofts it over cover for a SIX! FIRST MAXIMUM OF THE INNINGS!

That’s slightly quicker from Siddharth, Rahane hits it to long on for a single. Raghuvanshi hits it straight to cover, dot ball. Raghuvanshi tries to sweep it but it only goes to short fine leg, dot ball.

Shami with his second. Raghuvanshi on the up hits that straight to extra cover, dot ball.

Again on the up, this time its straight to cover, another dot ball. Raghuvanshi tries to guide that to deep third but it goes straight to Marsh at first slip. Three dot balls on the trot. Raghuvanshi creates room and goes inside out over cover for a FOUR!

Raghuvanshi defends that, dot ball. Slow and full from Shami, Raghuvanshi hits that straight but is intercepted at mid on by Rathi, keeps it down to a single.

Prince Yadav into the attack. Wide down the leg side from Prince, sharply outswings as it goes to the keeper. Allen hits that in the gap in deep square leg for a BOUNDARY!

Short from Prince and Allen pulls that past short fine leg for ANOTHER FOUR! Full from Prince, Allen hits that straight to extra cover, dot ball. Nine runs off the first three balls so far.

Oh Allen tries to pull that but top edges it straight to Digvesh Rathi at deep third who takes a good catch! ALLEN IS GONE EARLY! KKR ONE DOWN!

Finn Allen c Digvesh Singh Rathi b Prince Yadav 9(8) [4s-2]

Angkrish Raghuvanshi in at three for KKR, not Cameron Green. Gets off the mark with a single to deep point. Oooohh replays show Rathi’s foot was too close to the boundary line there. No run to finish.

Allen and Rahane walk out to open the batting for KKR. Shami takes the new ball for LSG. Rahane on strike, first slip in place. Outswinger from Shami to start, Rahane guides it to deep third for a single. Allen on strike.

Ooohhh play and a miss from Allen, Shami swings that wide outside off, dot ball. And again, sharp movement from Shami, another play and a miss from Allen, beats his outside edge. Allen charges and it’s an inswinger this time from Shami, beats the batter again. Three dot balls to start from Allen.

Allen edges that to short third for a single, fantastic first over from Shami so far. Down the ground for the first BOUNDARY FROM RAHANE!

KKR stumbled to 25/2 in 3.4 overs in the last match against Punjab Kings while batting first but can Rahane and Allen give KKR a better start today?

Or will LSG’s bowling, which has done a fine job in the first two games, be able to restrict KKR to less than 180?

This promises to be an exciting contest. IPL fans, are you ready? Here we go...

KKR: Manish Pandey, Vaibhav Arora, Tejasvi Singh, Rahul Tripathi, Tim Seifert

LSG: Ayush Badoni, Matthew Breetzke, Shahbaz Ahmad, Mayank Yadav, Himmat Singh

LSG: Mitchell Marsh, Aiden Markram, Rishabh Pant, Nicholas Pooran, Abdul Samad, Mukul Choudhary, M Siddharth, Avesh Khan, Mohammed Shami, Digvesh Rathi, Prince Yadav

KKR: Finn Allen, Ajinkya Rahane, Cam Green, Angkrish Raghuvanshi, Rovman Powell, Rinku Singh, Ramandeep Singh, Sunil Narine, Anukul Roy, Navdeep Saini, Kartik Tyagi

Lucknow Super Giants has won the toss and opts to bowl.

LSG is playing with the same team, says Rishabh Pant.

Sunil Narine is back in the playing XI in place of Blessing Muzarabani, says Ajinkya Rahane. He also confirms that Cam Green is ready to bowl.

Varun Chakaravarthy hasn’t marked his run-up yet. It seems he’s not playing today as well.

Cameron Green has marked his run-up. So, one can expect him to roll his arms over. He has been bowling in the training session as well.

There will be swing around. Although, with the rain around the city in the last few days including today, expect slowness in the field. Experts on the broadcast suggest the pitch being used today is usually a high-scoring one. But there’s a dilemma that the captains have: whether to bat first on a pitch that is likely to get slower through the game or chase looking at the weather and possible rain.

The most expensive player at the auction is long overdue for a big one and tonight could be the night that Green finally breaks through the shackles. The Australian was bought by the franchise to fill the big shoes left behind by Andre Russell and has unfortunately not done that job so far.

But with reports suggesting that Green will be back bowling in today’s game combined with his shortage of runs, the all-rounder will be eager to prove a point and silence the critics.

LSG defeated KKR by four runs in a high scoring contest. Batting first, LSG put up a mammoth 238 thanks to Nicholas Pooran’s unbeaten 87 and Mitchell Marsh’s 81. KKR did well to come close to the target, through captain Ajinkya Rahane’s 50 but in the end fell short by four runs.

Markram has been one of the most consistent performers with the bat around the world over the last few years but has not hit the ground running yet in the 2026 season.

The South African did score a 45 in the last game but by his lofty standards, anything less than a half century is considered a failure. Against a weak bowling attack in KKR and in a ground with small boundaries, can Markram score his first half century of the season?

Good news IPL fans, there’s no sign of rain. Clear skies and dry conditions currently at the Eden Gardens, and we’re all set for a full 20 over game.

We all know how Rahane likes to take on pacers, especially in the PowerPlay. Today, he will be up against Mohammed Shami. The two have faced each other in eight innings across T20s. Rahane has never got out to Shami and has scored 94 runs against the fast bowler at a strike rate of 164.91.

Mitchell Marsh, Aiden Markram, Rishabh Pant, Nicholas Pooran, Ayush Badoni, Abdul Samad, Mukul Choudhary, Shahbaz Ahmed, Digvesh Rathi, Anrich Nortje, Prince Yadav, Mohammed Shami

Ajinkya Rahane, Finn Allen, Angkrish Raghuvanshi, Cameron Green, Rinku Singh, Ramandeep Singh, Sunil Narine, Anukul Roy, Blessing Muzarabani, Navdeep Saini/ Varun Chakaravarthy, Vaibhav Arora, Kartik Tyagi

Nicholas Pooran has faced Sunil Narine in 27 innings across T20s over the years. Pooran has faced 144 balls and scored 193 runs at a strike rate of 134.02. Narine has dismissed him four times.

There have been reports stating that Cameron Green will be fit to take up bowling duties against LSG tonight and if that turns out to be true, it will be a big boost for KKR, which has barely been coping with a depleted bowling lineup. KKR will also hope that Green can rediscover his form with the bat starting tonight, as the Australian has struggled to score runs so far.

Ajinkya Rahane (C), Rinku Singh, Angkrish Raghuvanshi, Manish Pandey, Rovman Powell, Rahul Tripathi, Finn Allen, Tejasvi Singh, Tim Seifert, Sunil Narine, Anukul Roy, Ramandeep Singh, Cameron Green, Sarthak Ranjan, Daksh Kamra, Rachin Ravindra, Navdeep Saini, Vaibhav Arora, Umran Malik, Matheesha Pathirana, Kartik Tyagi, Akash Deep, Varun Chakaravarthy, Prashant Solanki.

Rishabh Pant (C), Aiden Markram, Himmat Singh, Matthew Breetzke, Mukul Choudhary, Akshat Raghuwanshi, Josh Inglis, Mitchell Marsh, Abdul Samad, Shahbaz Ahamad, Arshin Kulkarni, Wanindu Hasaranga, Ayush Badoni, Mohammad Shami, Avesh Khan, M. Siddharth, Digvesh Singh, Akash Singh, Prince Yadav, Arjun Tendulkar, Anrich Nortje, Naman Tiwari, Mayank Yadav, Mohsin Khan.

Quinton de Kock. The South African wicketkeeper-batter has scored 243 runs in five innings in contest between the two sides at a strike rate of 169.93 and an average of 60.75.

Since IPL 2023, Ajinkya Rahane has a strike rate of 119.93 against the spinners. However, he has been complete opposite against the pace bowlers, against whom he strikes at over 170.

Narine wasn’t well ahead of the game against Punjab and hence wasn’t available for that fixture. However, he has been training with the team for the last two days and is likely to be available today.

Varun Chakaravarthy injured his finger during the match against Sunrisers Hyderabad and has been nursing that since then. He remains doubtful starter for today as well.

Good news is that the precipitation chances during the match hours have reduced and we are likely in for a full game if these conditions prevail.

The last match at the Eden Gardens, between Kolkata Knight Riders and Punjab Kings, was abandoned after just 3.4 overs due to rain. Ahead of today’s match as well, there’s a rain forecast that could impact the proceedings, or at least the start of the contest.

Welcome to the live coverage of the IPL 2026 match between Kolkata Knight Riders and Lucknow Super Giants at the Eden Gardens in Kolkata. Stay tuned for the live updates.

The headline is misleading. Generation of 110 V does not imply energy, since energy is voltage times current. You cannot generate more electrical energy than the kinetic energy of the falling drop(s).
